text: This video depicts a totally laparoscopic Pusteau procedure.
Our patient's a forty-seven year old female with chronic pancreatitis secondary to alcoholism.
Her CT shows a dilated pancreatic duct with a stone at the head of the pancreas.
ERCP is considered, but with risk of stricture at the stone side, a PUSO procedure is chosen to alleviate her chronic pain.
This is the first totally laparoscopic PUSO procedure reported in literature without robotic assistance.
The video displays CT scan images showing a nearly one centimeter dilated pancreatic duct located near the tail of the pancreas.
The laparoscopic view reveals a 1.4 centimeter sized pancreatic duct located at the mid-body of the pancreas.
The CT scan image displays a stone located in the head of the pancreas, which is visually identifiable as the cause of chronic pancreatitis and pain.
The operation begins with laparoscopic trocars already inserted into the abdominal cavity, preparing for the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz located at the base of the transverse mesocolon during the laparoscopic pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon measures forty centimeters down from the identified anatomical landmark and transects the small bowel at this point.
The surgeon creates a side-to-side jejunojejunostomy after measuring 20 centimeters for the Roux limb, connecting two segments of the jejunum.
The surgeon creates a side-to-side anastomosis between two segments of the small intestine, with the bowel loops aligned and sutured together.
The surgeon closes the aneurotomy, followed by the closure of the mesenteric defect to prevent the formation of an internal hernia at the site of the jejujejunostomy.
The surgeon measures the roux limb to approximately twenty centimeters in length and positions it in a retrocolic fashion, preparing for further anastomosis.
The pancreas is fully exposed, and the pancreatic duct is visibly opened.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac at the greater curve of the stomach, detaching the greater omentum along the entire length of the greater curve to provide access to the pancreas.
The posterior wall of the stomach becomes visible, followed by the identification of the pancreas.
The pancreas is observed to be firm in texture, indicating its consistency during the procedure.
The pancreas is surrounded by significant inflammation, visibly attributed to chronic pancreatitis.
The surgeon demonstrates the firm texture of the pancreas and identifies the pancreatic duct. A needle is used to withdraw clear fluid from the duct, highlighting its location within the gland.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to incise along the anterior wall of the pancreas, following a specific angle to access the pancreatic duct.
The surgeon continues the ductotomy on the pancreas, following the previously established incision line to further access the pancreatic duct.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the tail of the pancreas, following the established ductotomy path.
The Maryland dissector is used to access the pancreatic duct, visible within the firm and inflamed pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to open the pancreatic duct, which is visible within the firm texture of the pancreatic gland.
The surgeon opens the wide channel of the pancreatic duct, extending the incision towards the tail of the pancreas, revealing the duct's interior.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to incise through the firm anterior wall of the pancreatic gland, demonstrating the gland's dense texture.
It is done with great difficulty due to the firm texture.
The surgeon positions the roux limb in a retrocolic fashion, orienting the blind end towards the tail of the pancreas.
The surgeon examines the pancreatic ductotomy, which extends visibly all the way to the tail of the pancreas.
The surgeon initiates an enterotomy in the roux limb, preparing it for anastomosis with the pancreatic duct.
The surgeon extends the incision along the anterior wall of the pancreas using hook cautery, demonstrating the firm texture of the gland.
The surgeon examines the pancreatic ductotomy, which extends towards the tail of the pancreas, noting the firm texture of the tissue.
The surgeon prepares to perform an anastomosis between the pancreatic duct and the small bowel, following the complete opening of the pancreatic ductotomy.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis of the pancreatic duct to the small bowel using a running PDS suture, visible as the suture is continuously applied along the ductotomy site.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to secure the anastomosis between the pancreatic duct and the small bowel.
The surgeon uses a unidirectionally barbed suture, which locks the tissue in place as it is pulled through, preventing slack in the sutured area.
The surgeon anchors the corner at the tail of the pancreas, threading the suture through its own looped end and pulling it taut to secure the tissue.
The surgeon secures the suture in place using the barbs, ensuring the tissue remains taut without slack.
The surgeon begins the anastomosis using a running suture technique, starting from the tail of the pancreas and proceeding along the inferior border of the ductotomy.
The surgeon attempts to drive the needle through the firm tissue of the pancreas, highlighting the resistance encountered during the suturing process.
The surgeon encounters resistance while attempting to drive the needle through the stiff pancreatic tissue during the suturing process.
The surgeon pulls the suture taut, utilizing the barbed nature of the suture to securely lock the small bowel against the pancreas.

The surgeon extends the pancreatic ductotomy towards the head of the pancreas by a few centimeters to address the size mismatch between the small bowel and the pancreatic ductotomy, ensuring alignment with the aneurotomy length.
The surgeon continues the anastomosis by taking full-thickness bites through both the small bowel and the pancreatic wall, ensuring secure attachment of the two structures.

The surgeon reaches the apex of the inferior wall of the pancreas and threads the suture back through the small bowel, utilizing the barb nature of the suture to securely lock the small bowel against the pancreas.
The surgeon cuts the suture to the appropriate length and holds it up to maintain orientation during the procedure.
The surgeon encounters needle trauma while driving the needle through the calcified pancreatic wall, visible as resistance during the suturing process.
The video shows the firm texture of the pancreas, highlighting its dense and solid appearance.
The surgeon uses the barbed suture technique to secure the sutures in place, ensuring they remain stable within the pancreatic wall and small bowel.
The surgeon performs the superior aspect of the anastomosis by running a second suture from the tail of the pancreas, attaching the small bowel to the pancreatic wall.
The surgeon reaches the apex of the anastomosis, crossing the sutures to ensure the two suture lines overlap, maintaining alignment between the pancreatic tissue and the small bowel.
We will then clip
The surgeon secures the suture in place, completing the attachment of the small bowel to the pancreatic wall during the anastomosis.
We do not need to tie them.
The surgeon demonstrates the barb nature of the suture, which visibly locks itself in place during the anastomosis between the tail of the pancreas and the small bowel.
The surgeon tacks the Roux limb to the transverse mesocolon, securing it to prevent the formation of internal hernias.
Patient had uncomplicated post-operative course.
She was advanced to clear as on post-op day one, regular diet at post-op day two, and discharged on post-op day four.
A drain had been left in place in the operating room, and this strain amylase was a level of eighteen post-operatively and was pulled on post-operative day ten.
Six weeks after the operation, the patient was pain-free.
Thank you.
